summary: The review of the Master Services Agreement Example 1 (1).pdf indicates a moderate risk posture overall, with several clauses flagged for misalignment with the University of Auckland's Preferred Contracting Positions. Key issues include governing law deviations, overly broad indemnities, and problematic termination provisions, particularly unilateral termination for convenience and insufficient safeguards for accrued rights. Immediate escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts is recommended to negotiate amendments to high-risk clauses and ensure compliance with UoA's policy framework.

flags.clause_title: 20 TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE
flags.snippet: 20 TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE
flags.rationale: The clause explicitly introduces termination for convenience, which is not aligned with UoA's Preferred Position for termination clauses. Termination for convenience is only acceptable for Consulting contracts under specific conditions (reasonable notice, PI agreement, and client paying termination costs/unavoidable obligations). This clause does not provide any of these safeguards, nor does it specify the contract type or conditions under which termination for convenience applies. Escalation to the Senior Manager Research Grants and Contracts is required to assess whether this clause can be negotiated or amended.
flags.text: 20 TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE

flags.clause_title: 20.1 The Service Provider may terminate this Agreement by giving Notice to
flags.snippet: 20.1 The Service Provider may terminate this Agreement by giving Notice to that:
flags.rationale: The clause is incomplete and does not specify the grounds for termination, the notice period, or any obligations related to termination costs or uncancellable obligations. This materially deviates from UoA's Preferred Position #POS-19, which requires mutual two-way termination for material breach or insolvency, clear rights for UoA to terminate for non-payment, and client responsibility for termination costs. The clause also fails to align with the Acceptable tier, as it does not address termination for convenience with reasonable notice and PI agreement. Escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts is required.

flags.clause_title: 22 CONFIDENTIALITY
flags.snippet: 22 CONFIDENTIALITY
flags.rationale: The clause text provided ('22 CONFIDENTIALITY') is insufficient to determine alignment with UoA Position #POS-11. Confidentiality clauses must explicitly exclude information already public, independently developed, previously known by the receiving party, and information legally required to be disclosed. Additionally, UoA must retain the ability to disclose to agents and Affiliates, and the duration of confidentiality obligations must be limited to up to 5 years post-completion. Without further detail, this clause cannot be confirmed as compliant and requires escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts for review.

Additionally, it does not specify UoA's ability to disclose to agents and Affiliates, nor does it limit the confidentiality obligations to a defined duration (e.g., up to 5 years post-completion). These omissions materially deviate from the Preferred tier and require escalation to the Senior Manager, Research Grants and Contracts for review.

flags.snippet: C. The Services provided under this Agreement will be delivered in the Partner Country, which is a jurisdiction outside of Australia. The Parties have agreed that Laws of the Partner Country that govern or impose Tax, w…
flags.rationale: The clause specifies that the Services will be delivered in a jurisdiction outside of Australia (the 'Partner Country') and that the laws of the Partner Country will apply to certain obligations. This falls outside the UoA Preferred and Acceptable tiers for Governing Law and Jurisdiction (POS-16), which only allow New Zealand, Australia, the United Kingdom, Common European countries, Singapore, or non-exclusive jurisdiction of a specific US state. The clause also introduces dual applicability of laws (Partner Country and Australian laws), which complicates compliance and interpretation. Escalation to General Counsel is required to assess the risks and determine whether this arrangement can be negotiated or accepted.

flags.snippet: 20.5 If terminates this Agreement and/or a Work Order under clause 20.4, will only be liable to the Service Provider for the following loss or damage incurred as a direct consequence of termination of this Agreement to the extent that they can be reasonably substantiated and are unable to be avoided or mitigated:
flags.rationale: The clause addresses termination-related liability, which is a UoA-sensitive topic under Position #POS-19. While it limits liability to direct, substantiated, and unavoidable/mitigated losses, it does not explicitly align with the Preferred Position requiring mutual two-way termination for material breach or insolvency, nor does it confirm the client's obligation to pay termination costs, including uncancellable obligations. However, it does not introduce unacceptable terms such as termination for convenience without safeguards. This places the clause in the Acceptable tier, but it requires confirmation by the Contract Manager to ensure alignment with UoA's broader termination provisions.

18 FORCE MAJEURE
18.1 “Force Majeure Event” is limited to the following specific events or circumstances:
earthquake, landslide, fire, explosion, war, invasion, act of foreign enemies, hostilities, civil
war, rebellion, revolution, insurrection, military or usurped power, martial law or confiscation
by order of any government or authority, ionising radiations or contamination by radioactivity
from any nuclear fuel or from any nuclear waste from the combustion of nuclear fuel, riot, civil
disturbance, blockade or acts of terrorism, pandemic or epidemic which prevents a Party (“the
Affected Party”) from complying with any of its obligations under this Agreement and which
that the Affected Party:
(a) did not cause or contribute to (by breach of this Agreement or otherwise);
(b) cannot reasonably control or influence; and
(c) where the Affected Party is the Service Provider, cannot be prevented or avoided or
overcome, through prudent management processes, policies and precautions,
including the use of alternative resources, the procuring of services from another
source and work around plans.
18.2 If either party is prevented from performing any obligations under this Agreement by a Force
Majeure Event, then provided that the Affected Party has complied with clause 19.3 such
obligations will be suspended, and the Affected Party will have no liability to the other party for
failure to perform them, to the extent their performance is prevented by the Force Majeure
Event.
18.3 The Affected Party must:
(a) notify the other Party immediately if a Force Majeure Event is preventing it, or is likely
to prevent it, from complying with any of its obligations as soon as it becomes aware
of the Force Majeure Event and provide full details of the Force Majeure Event
including:
(1) the obligations affected;
(2) the nature, extent and likely duration of the effect on those obligations; and
(3) the actions taken or proposed to be taken by the Affected Party to remedy,
abate, mitigate or minimise the effects of the Force Majeure Event;
(b) use all reasonable diligence and means to remedy, abate, mitigate or minimise the
effect of the Force Majeure Event;
(c) notify the other party in writing when resumption of performance can occur; and
(d) promptly resume performance (and give notice of such resumption) as soon as
reasonably possible.
18.4 The Affected Party must bear its own costs incurred in connection with a Force Majeure Event
and the other Party is not liable for (and the Affected Party may not make) any claim or
demand relating to a Force Majeure Event.
